segments.s700-g105: ازان آگهی is a temporal/causal adjunct: ازان (= از + آن, “from/thereupon that”) + آگهی (“news/information”), so ‘upon that news’. سر به سر is an idiomatic compound (‘head to head’) meaning ‘one and all / entirely’, followed by نو (adjective ‘new’) and the past plural verb شدند (‘they became / were’). The overt subject is omitted and recovered from the plural verb (they — i.e. the people just mentioned).
segments.s700-g106: بیاری here functions adverbially/compatibly as ‘with (their) companions’ (an older way to express accompaniment). به نزدیک + [subject] + شدند uses به + adjective نزدیک (‘near’) with the past plural شدن to form نزدیک شدن (‘to approach’), so خسرو is the goal ('to Khosrow') and شدند marks the past plural (‘they came/they drew near’). Word order places the accompaniment (بیاری) before the motion clause for emphasis.
segments.s700-g97: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is a simple past ('came'). به لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the army'. گه خویش is an archaic/poetic phrasing meaning 'at his (own) time/occasion' (گه = گاه, and خوی + enclitic ش = 'his'), and باز after it makes the idea 'again/returned' (so the whole segment = 'when he again came to his army at his accustomed hour').
segments.s700-g98: همان is 'that same'. تیره here means the ominous/dark mood. گشت is the past of شدن 'became'. آن شب دیریاز is a poetic nominal phrase 'that night (آن شب) long-lasting/extended (دیریاز)'; together: 'the same gloom fell — that night became long.'
segments.s700-g99: فرستاد is the past transitive 'he sent'. بیدار is an adjective before the noun meaning 'awake/alert'. کارآگهان (archaic plural form of کارآگه) means 'the informed/alert men/scouts'; the whole NP is the direct object of فرستاد: 'he sent alert scouts/informants.'
segments.s700-g100: که تا here introduces purpose/result: queuing 'so that / in order that'. باز جویند is a present-subjunctive-style phrase meaning 'they may search again' (جویند = they seek). کارجهان is a compound/poetic word (literally 'کارِ جهان') meaning 'the affairs/news of the world' — the object of جویند. Overall: 'so that they should again seek news/affairs of the world.'
segments.s700-g101: چو is the temporal 'when'. آگاه شد is a verbal phrase 'became aware / was informed' (آگاه as adjective/noun + شد). لشکر نیمروز is the subject 'the army/host of Nimruz' (نیمروز here the regional name).
segments.s700-g102: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. آمد is past 'came'. ز ره is the poetic preposition + noun 'from/by the road/way'. شاه گیتی فروز is a titular noun phrase 'the king who lights/illuminates the world' (گیتی‌فروز as epithet); the clause says that this king came by the road.
segments.s700-g103: همه is 'all/everyone'. کوس بستند is a fixed poetic expression: بستند here can mean 'fastened' or idiomatically 'beat/sounded (the) drums' — بستند is past plural. بر پشت پیل is the locative phrase 'upon the elephants' backs' (بر = on, پشت = back, پیل = elephant).
segments.s700-g104: زمین شد is 'the earth/ground became'. به کردار is an idiom 'in the manner/likeness of'. دریای نیل is 'the sea of the Nile' (دریا = sea, نیل = Nile). So the clause means 'the land was transformed, in appearance, into the Nile's sea' (a poetic image of vastness or flooding by people/elephants).
segments.s700-g17: بجایی که موسیل بود ارمنی is an introductory location clause: بجایی که (بجایِ + که) = 'at the place where' (poetic/compound locative), موسیل بود is a past-tense copula clause 'Mosul was/stood', and ارمنی apposes or predicates someone introduced next (an Armenian). Word order is locative phrase → relative clause → predicate; the past tense بود marks the setting.
segments.s700-g18: که introduces a relative clause modifying ارمنی: 'who/that'. کردی here functions as an ethnic/agentive label 'Kurd(ish)' rather than the verb 'you did', and میان بزرگان منی is an existential/location phrase 'among my great ones' with منی (من + the enclitic ی) marking possession/existence. The clause ends the predication with the understood copula 'was' (the existence is asserted of that Armenian among the speaker's great men).
segments.s700-g19: به لشکر گهش یار بندوی بود is a locative/existential clause: به لشکر = 'in/among the host', گهش (a poetic adverb, 'occasionally/also') qualifies the following, یار بندوی = 'his companion, the بندوی (a devotee/slave/follower or proper epithet)', and بود is the past copula 'there was'. The noun phrase is the subject of بود.
segments.s700-g20: که introduces a defining relative clause about that بندوی: 'who...'. بندوی خال جهانجوی is a sequence of epithets/names (بندوی + خال + جهانجوی) — خال here functions as part of a name/epithet and جهانجوی is an adjectival/agentive compound 'world-seeking/ambitious'. The clause closes with بود, the past copula 'was'.
segments.s700-g21: برفت این دوگرد ازمیان سپاه is a past action clause: برفت (poetic past of رفت, 'went/rode away') is the verbal nucleus, این دوگرد = 'these two (turns/riders/circuits)' is the subject phrase, and ازمیان سپاه = 'from the midst of the host' is the source phrase. Word order is verb-first (poetic) followed by the subject phrase and source.
segments.s700-g22: ز لشکر نگه کرد خسرو به راه is a coordinated clause: ز لشکر = 'from/of the army' (poetic z = from/of), نگه کرد is a compound verb 'kept/held watch' (نگه + کرد where کرد is the light verb), خسرو is the agent/subject 'Khosrow', and به راه gives the scope/direction 'on/toward the road'. The order places the object/source before the verb for emphasis, typical of epic diction.
segments.s700-g23: به گستهم گفت آن دلاور دومرد is an addressed-speech frame: به گستهم = 'to Gostaham' (به marks the indirect object/recipient), گفت is the past verb 'said', and آن دلاور دومرد is the nominative phrase identifying the speaker 'that brave (one), two-men' — i.e. 'the brave man said' or 'the brave man, of the two'. The clause sets up direct speech that follows.
segments.s700-g24: چنین اسپ تازان به دشت نبرد is the reported content: چنین = 'thus/so', اسپ تازان is a noun phrase 'steeds/horsemen that are تازان' (تازان functioning as a participial/epithet 'swift/fierce/charging'), به دشت = 'into the plain', and نبرد is used as the event 'battle' (either noun 'battle' or verb sense 'to fight' understood elliptically). The sequence describes the two horsemen charging into the plain to fight.
segments.s700-g73: برون + کرد forms a verbal phrase where برون (an adverb 'out/forth') precedes the verb کرد ('made/did') to mean 'put out' or 'thrust out'. یک پای خویش is the direct object 'one foot of his' (خویش split into خوی + ش here), and از رکیب is a prepositional phrase 'from the rākib/saddle/steed' indicating source; word order is slightly inverted in poetic diction but functions as Subject‑Verb/Object/Prepositional phrase.
segments.s700-g74: شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became' placed before the nominal phrase (poetic inversion). آن مرد is the subject 'that man', and بیدار دل ناشکیب is a stacked adjective phrase: بیدار دل 'wakeful/alert of heart' with ناشکیب 'impatient' qualifying the same subject.
segments.s700-g75: ببوسید is the simple past 'he kissed'. پای و رکیب are two coordinated objects ('the foot and the rākib/companion'), and the clitic ورا after رکیب marks the indirect/object pronoun 'to him' (i.e. 'kissed him / kissed the companion'). The verb governs both coordinated objects in a terse poetic sequence.
segments.s700-g76: همی before a verb gives a continuous or emphatic aspect ('was/kept'), so همی خیره گشت means 'became/stood astonished'. از نهیب ورا is a causal/agentive phrase 'because of / from the rebuke directed at him' (ورا = 'to him'). The main verb گشت ('became') links the state to its cause.
segments.s700-g77: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the circumstance. بیکار شد is a verb phrase 'became idle/stood idle' and مرد خسروپرست is the noun phrase 'the man who worships Khosrow' (postposed after the predicate in poetic order).
segments.s700-g78: فرمود is 'he said/commanded'; فرمود تا introduces a subordinate clause of command/purpose ('ordered that...'). بر + نشست is a common compound meaning 'to sit down' (بر as prepositional element + نشست 'sit'). جهانجوی is the agent who issues the command.
segments.s700-g79: وزان (poetic contraction of 'و از آن') means 'and/from that', so وزان دشت = 'from that plain'. بی‌بر is an epithet modifying اسپ (here 'the ill‑fated' or similarly pejorative epithet), and انگیخت is the past 'roused/rose up' with اسپ as the subject (poetic word order places modifiers before the verb).
segments.s700-g80: همی‌تاخت is an imperfective/past progressive 'was charging' or 'rushed on'. تا here marks extent 'as far as/until', and پیش آذر گشسپ means 'before/in the presence of Azar Goshasp' (آذر is used as the proper name element and گشسپ is the hero's name).
segments.s700-g33: کجاکار بندوی is a noun phrase (کجاکار 'wrongdoer' modifying بندوی 'bondman/servant'). باشد is the literary present/subjunctive form of 'to be' (here rendering a general or potential statement: 'would be / is'), and درشت is the predicate adjective 'coarse, rude'. Word order is noun phrase → copula → predicate adjective, typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s700-g34: مگر introduces an exception or conditional idea ('unless' / 'except'). پاک یزدان is a genitive phrase ('pure God'); بود is the past/contracted form of 'to be' here used as the copula ('were / was'); یاروپشت is a compound noun meaning 'ally, companion and support.' Together the clause gives the exception: no coarse bondman would be so, unless the pure God were his supporter.
segments.s700-g35: اگر ... خواهی is a conditional (اگر = 'if'). زنده خواهی here means 'if you want to be alive / if you wish to live' (خواهی = 'you wish / desire'). به زندان بود is a terse instruction with به + place 'in/into prison' and بود as the copula/verb 'be' used here in a hortative/optative sense: 'be in prison.'
segments.s700-g36: وگر = 'but if / and if otherwise'. کشته is a past participle 'killed / to be slain' describing the eventuality. بردار is an imperative 'take!' (here 'take up' / 'go take'), and میدان is the place 'field / battlefield'; بود again acts as the copula/optative. The clause offers the converse option: if you are to be killed, go take the field.
segments.s700-g37: بدو گفت گستهم is inverted word order common in poetry: بدو 'to him', گفت 'said' (past 3rd), گستهم is the speaker's name (Gustaham). شاها is the vocative 'O king', and درست is an admonition/adjective 'be straight/true/just' addressed to the king. In short: 'Gustaham said to him, "O king, be right."'
segments.s700-g38: ب + دان here form the compound بدان (imperative/command) 'know this' (ب as prefix + دان 'know'). سونگه کن is an imperative phrase: سونگه (object: 'muzzle/point') + کن 'do / strike' = 'strike/aim at that muzzle'. که introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that...', اوخال 'that mole / beauty spot' is the object, and تست (unfortunately printed here in the list with senses relating to 'test') functions in the poem as a copula-like ending meaning 'is yours' (archaic spelling of توست). Because the provided senses for تست do not match its usage here, no provided sense fits.
segments.s700-g39: گرآید is a conditional/optative 'if he should come / if he comes'. به نزدیک = 'near' (prepositional phrase), وباشد is the conjunction + subjunctive/present copula 'and there be / and it be', and جزاوی is an adjective/noun meaning 'retributive / punishment' (here indicating that retribution/punishment is present). The clause sets the conditional scene: 'If he comes near and punishment occurs...'.
segments.s700-g40: ز = poetic 'from / by / because of'. گستهم here is the proper name Gustaham (though the lemma also would read as the verb 'I wandered'); گوینده 'the speaker' is an appositive describing Gustaham. جز = 'except'; جان = 'life'; مجوی is an instruction 'do not seek / do not ask'. Together: 'Do not ask anything from Gustaham the speaker except life.'
segments.s700-g9: This is a nominal phrase (no finite verb): 'سرا پرده شاه' = 'the king's pavilion/tent' followed by a locative expression. بردشت here is orthographically fused and functions like برِ دشت ('on the plain'); word order is Possessed + Possessor (سرا پردهِ شاه). The final دوک is a noun placed after the locative phrase, its exact semantic role is ambiguous in this line (poetic/elliptical).
segments.s700-g10: This is a second nominal/clausal description linked to the previous phrase: چنان لشکری = 'such an army'; گشن و راهی = two adjectives coordinated ('hungry' and 'on the road' / 'way-worn'); سه دوک remains a trailing noun phrase (poetic quantifier or descriptor). No overt verb appears here — the line strings descriptive nouns and adjectives in classical Persian, relying on the poem's sentence-level verb elsewhere.
segments.s700-g11: This is a compact transitive clause with the object-marked recipient first: 'نیاطوس را' (Niyātus + را marks him as definite object/recipient), then the verb 'داد' (gave), followed by 'لشکر همه' = 'the entire army' (لشکر modified by همه). Despite unusual order for modern Persian, in older/poetic Persian such fronting of the recipient is normal.
segments.s700-g12: بدو گفت = 'they/one said to him' (بدو = به او, 'to him'); گفت here is the reporting verb (past). 'مهتر تویی' is direct speech embedded after گفت, with تویی = 'thou art' (present/persistent copula in older Persian). بررمه is a genitival/qualifying noun ('of the host' / 'of the multitude') following مهتر. Word order: indirect object phrase + reporting verb + quoted predication.
segments.s700-g13: This adverbial clause sets scene/location: 'وزان' = 'and/from that', 'جایگه' = 'place' (object of وزان), then an accompanying phrase 'با سواران' = 'with the horsemen' and 'گرد' = 'around/about'. Word order is adverbial/place + accompaniment; it prepares the circumstance for the main action in the next segment.
segments.s700-g14: This contains the transitive action: عنان ... راسپرد corresponds to عنان را (the reins) + سپرد (handed over) in more regular Persian. Here راسپرد is the fused past participle 'handed over', and the intervening token (باره) functions as an archaic prepositional/relational element marking the recipient (i.e. 'to Tiztag'); تیزتگ appears in the syntactic slot of the indirect object (recipient). In sum: 'he handed the reins to Tiztag.'
segments.s700-g15: سوی راه = 'toward the road' (directional prepositional phrase). چیچست is an archaic interrogative/particle (here best read as a poetic exclamation or rhetorical 'what is it'—often functioning as a linking particle). بنهاد (past of نهادن) here means 'placed/turned' and combines with روی = 'face' to mean 'he turned his face toward' (بنهاد روی = 'set his face'). Word order: directional phrase + (particle) + verb phrase.
segments.s700-g16: همی‌راند is the imperfective/progressive past (همی- prefix + راندن) meaning 'he rode/was riding on'; this older progressive marker shows continuous action. 'شادان دل' = 'with rejoicing/joyful heart' (adverbial adjective + noun). 'وراه جوی' is a compound: وراه = 'and/on the road' or 'toward the road' and جوی from جویدن/جوییدن meaning 'seeking' here (participle-like), so together 'and seeking the way'. The clause describes his manner of riding.
segments.s700-g49: وزان is a fused adverbial (و + ز آن) meaning 'from that / from that time' and sets a temporal/casual frame. چاره جستن is an infinitive noun-phrase 'to seek a remedy' used narratively (classical/poetic Persian often uses the infinitive as the action in past narrative); دران روزگار (در آن روزگار) is a locative/time phrase 'in that time / in those days' modifying جستن. Word order here places the adverbial first, then the action expressed by an infinitive phrase.
segments.s700-g50: Again وزان ('and/from that time') ties this clause to the previous one. پوشش is the head noun 'covering; cloak'; جامه شهریار is a genitival phrase 'the king's garment/robe' placed after پوشش as its specification. Together the phrase says 'from that (time) the king's robe was (his) covering' — an elliptical, poetic nominal clause with the predicate implied.
segments.s700-g51: همی‌گفت is an archaic/imperfective verbal form (همی + گفت) meaning 'kept saying' or 'was saying' — the همی prefix gives continuous/habitual aspect. و connects the clause to the next: خسرو is the subject and فراوان is an adverb modifying گریست, where گریست is the past simple 'he wept.' Word order is standard narrative: verb/verb-phrase then coordinated clause with subject + adverb + verb.
segments.s700-g52: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereupon.' بدو گفت is 'said to him' (بدو = به + او, dative 'to him'; گفت is past 'he said') and introduces direct speech. کاین is the demonstrative/introductory particle 'that/this' used to point to the person, and مردکیست is the interrogative clause 'who is the man?' — here compressed into a literary form used as quoted question.
segments.s700-g53: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'); کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone. شاه is the addressee 'king' and خورشید چهر is a descriptive compound literally 'sun-face' (i.e. 'sun-faced' or 'radiant-faced') placed after شاه as an epithet. This is a formal poetic address combining vocative + title + epithet.
segments.s700-g54: تو موسیل را = 'you [about] Mosul' with را marking موسیل as the topical/object phrase. چون here functions as 'how/why' (a rhetorical interrogative). نپرسی is negative + present-subjunctive/indicative form of پرسیدن (ن + پرسی) giving 'do you not ask / why don't you ask' — in classical style the negative present can form a rhetorical question. ز مهر = 'about/from (your) favour/affection' (ز = 'about/from' in poetry; مهر = 'favour/kindness'), completing the question 'why don't you ask Mosul about the favour?'
segments.s700-g55: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/since'). تا here has the temporal sense 'when/until' marking the time boundary. تو ز ایران شدستی is an archaic phrasing meaning 'you left/you were from Iran' — شدستی is an older second-person past/compound form (classical inflection) used here to record the event, and بروم ('I went') is placed after to indicate the speaker's action following that event; together the clause states 'when you left Iran, I went away.'
segments.s700-g56: نخفتست is a past perfective/poetic negative-form meaning 'did not sleep / has not slept' (ن + خفتست), here describing ongoing sleeplessness. هرگز 'never' strengthens the negation. بآباد qualifies بوم (ب + آباد = 'prospered/inhabited'), and بوم is 'dwelling/abode' or 'land'; together بآباد بوم = 'prosperous abode/land.' The clause states that the prosperous home has never known rest/sleep since that time.
segments.s700-g1: بهشتم is a poetic locative/possessive phrase ('behesht' + -am) meaning 'my paradise/heaven' and functions as the thing being adorned. بیاراست is a past tense transitive verb ('he adorned' or 'made beautiful') taking that phrase as its object. خورشید چهر is a nominal compound 'sun‑face' (literally 'sun face' = 'sun‑like visage') placed after the verb as the instrument/quality with which the paradise is adorned.
segments.s700-g2: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army/host' as a definite object. بکردار is a predicative/verbalized adjective meaning 'made (them) have form/virtue' or 'rendered comely/ordered' (causative sense). گردان سپهر is an epithet/phrase ('turning/revolving heaven') that here qualifies the agent (the sun/heavenly mover) or acts as a poetic epithet attached to the preceding action.
segments.s700-g3: ز درگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the court/threshold' placed at the clause start for setting the scene. برخاست is the past intransitive verb 'rose/ascended'. آوای کوس is a genitive construction ('the sound/voice of the drum'); آوای carries the ezāfe relation to کوس and functions as the subject/theme of برخاست.
segments.s700-g4: هواشد is a compound past meaning 'was set in motion/was roused' (literally 'became in the air/was moved'), used here to report the army's reaction. زگرد is a poetic preposition 'from around' introducing the source/location. سپاه آبنوس is a noun phrase 'the ebony/very dark host' (سپاه modified by آبنوس); آبنوس is an adjectival description of the army.
segments.s700-g5: سپاهی here means 'soldiers/troops' and is the thing selected. گزین کرد is a compound past formed by the verbal stem گزین ('choose') plus the light verb کرد ('did/made'), yielding 'he chose' or 'he selected'. زآزادگان is a prepositional phrase 'from the freeborn/nobles' indicating the source of the selected troops.
segments.s700-g6: بیامد is the simple past 'he/they came'. سوی is the preposition 'toward' marking direction, and آذرابادگان is the place name 'Azarabadagan' as the goal of motion. The clause continues the action of the previous clause (the chosen troops moved toward that place).
segments.s700-g7: دو هفته is a temporal phrase 'two weeks' indicating duration and appears before the verb (typical in Persian). برآمد is the past verb 'arose/appeared/mustered' describing the army's emergence. بفرمان شاه is an agentive prepositional phrase 'by the king's command' (ب-فرمان + agent شاه) showing authority for the action.
segments.s700-g8: بلشکر is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'with an army' (or 'in multitudes') setting accompaniment. گه آمد literally 'at times came' (گه as temporal/adverbial 'occasionally' or 'then'), while دمادم is an adverb 'continuously/again and again' modifying آمد; سپاه is the noun 'the host/army' that performs the coming. Together they report repeated movement of the host.
segments.s700-g25: 'برو' is the simple imperative 'go'; it is followed by the directional preposition 'سوی' + object 'ایشان' ('toward them'). 'ببین' is another imperative 'look/see' used as a hortative 'go and see', and 'تاکیند' is an interrogative/temporal phrase (classical orthography of تا کی‌اند) meaning 'until/for how long' or 'how long are they' — here it completes the indirect question introduced by 'ببین' (i.e. 'go and see how long they...').
segments.s700-g26: 'برین گونه' is the fused form of 'بر این گونه' and functions adverbially as 'in this manner/way' (note the old orthography: 'بر' + 'ین' = 'این'). 'تازان' is the plural noun 'raiders/horse-chargers' and 'زبهر' ('زِ + بهر') means 'for/on account of' or 'for the attack'; 'چیند' (classical present 'چینند' spelled 'چیند') is a 3rd-person plural present meaning 'they arrange/gather/draw up' — together: 'in this way the raiders are assembling.'
segments.s700-g27: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus said' (typical poetic word order: verb phrase before subject). 'گستهم' here is the speaker's name (Gostaham) in the nominative; 'کای شهریار' is a vocative phrase 'O king' with 'کای' the archaic vocative particle and 'شهریار' 'king.'
segments.s700-g28: 'برانم' is first-person verbal form with the suffix -م, functioning as a volitive/performative 'let me/I will drive (away).' 'که' here links the verb to its object clause ('that/whom'); 'آن مرد ابلق سوار' is the object: demonstrative 'that' + 'مرد' (man/hero) with the adjective 'ابلق' (piebald) and the noun 'سوار' (horseman) in apposition — 'that piebald horseman.'
segments.s700-g29: برادرم is the subject with the first-person possessive clitic (-م) = “my brother.” بندوی is a predicate-nominal/object meaning “a bondman/servant.” کنداورست is an archaic past tense verbal phrase (here parsed as کند آورست) functioning as the 3rd-person past: “(he) made/turned (him).” Word order is subject – predicate/object – verb (typical older Persian narrative order).
segments.s700-g30: همان یارش = “that very companion/friend of his,” with همان as a demonstrative emphasizing the following noun. ازلشکری is a compound meaning “of the army / belonging to an army” (literally ‘from the soldiery’) and functions as an adjectival/nominal descriptor. دیگرست is the 3rd-person copula (older form) meaning “is other/another” → “belongs to a different army.”
segments.s700-g31: چنین is an adverb “thus/so.” گفت is the narrative past “said” with خسرو as its subject (خسرو گفت = “Khosrow said”). بگستهم + شیر here is an archaic/poetic phrase: شیر is a vocative or descriptive epithet “lion” and بگستهم is an older prepositional/verb form tied to the speech-act (rendered idiomatically as “like a lion” or ‘with lion-like voice’). The whole chunk frames who is speaking and in what manner.
segments.s700-g32: که here introduces an indirect/direct question (“who …?”). این کی بود = “who was this?” with کی used in the sense “who” (classical usage) and بود the past copula. ای سوار دلیر is a vocative after the question: ای = “O!”, سوار = “rider/horseman,” دلیر = “brave.” The clause thus addresses the rider directly while asking the identity of the person.
segments.s700-g65: بکوشیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive ('let us strive'). تا introduces the purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). روز تو به شود is the subordinate clause: روز ('day') + تو (your) followed by به شود, where شود is present-subjunctive/passive-style 'become' and به functions here adverbially ('well' / 'good') — together 'that your day become good.' Word order places the verb at the end, as normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s700-g66: همان نامت = 'that very name of yours' (همان as 'that/that very', followed by نامت 'your name/reputation'). از introduces the domain ('among/of the ...') and مهتران here means 'the greater/leading ones' (a plural reference to great men). مه is used metaphorically as 'moon' and شود is the subjunctive 'become' — overall: 'may your name become a moon among the great.'
segments.s700-g67: بدو گفت is an inverted datival construction: بدو 'to him' (dative) + گفت (past 'said') — this order is typical in older Persian prose/poetry. موسیل is the speaker's name (here the subject); کای شهریار is a vocative phrase addressing the sovereign ('O King' or 'Kay, sovereign').
segments.s700-g68: بمن 'to me' marks the beneficiary. بریکی is the item requested (lit. 'a small/delicate [thing]'); تازه کن is an imperative expression 'make fresh/renew' (تازه + کن 'do/make'), and روزگار here means 'fate/fortune.' The verb کن (imperative) governs روزگار as the thing to be renewed: 'renew my fortunes for me.'
segments.s700-g69: که introduces a purposive/result clause ('so that/in order that'). آیم and ببوسم are 1st-person singular present-subjunctive verbs stacked to express the speaker's intended actions ('I may come, I may kiss'); Persian often strings verbs like this before or after the object. رکیب تو را is the definite object (تو 'your' + را object marker) placed before the verbs for poetic effect.
segments.s700-g70: ستایش کنم is another 1st-person-present-subjunctive clause ('I may praise' / 'I will praise'); here it continues the series of intended acts begun by که. فر و زیب تو را lists two coordinated direct objects ('your glory and beauty') with a single final را marking definiteness/emphasis for the whole phrase.
segments.s700-g71: بدو گفت خسرو follows the same datival inversion: بدو 'to him' + گفت (said) + خسرو (Khosrow) as the speaker. که begins the content clause. با رنج تو is a prepositional phrase 'with/through your hardship' (با = 'with', رنج = 'suffering/hardship', تو = 'your') that modifies what follows.
segments.s700-g72: درفشان کنم is a verb phrase: درفشان (here adjectival/participial 'shining, glittering') + کنم 'I will make/do', so 'I will make [it] shine.' زین سخن is an instrumental/genitive phrase 'by/from this speech' (زین = 'from this' + سخن 'word/saying'), and گنج تو 'your treasure' is the direct object. The clause thus expresses Khosrow's promise to make the addressee's treasure shine through these words.
segments.s700-g89: همی‌گفت is an iterative past/continuous narrative verb meaning “kept saying.” کای is the vocative particle “O,” addressing someone. داور is a noun here, “arbiter/judge,” followed by داد as a noun “justice” (an epithet: “just”), and وپاک is a compound adjective “and pure/clean”; the whole segment is a vocative formula (“O just and pure Arbiter”).
segments.s700-g90: This is the request/clause that follows the vocative: سردشمنان is the direct object “(the) enemy‑leaders.” اندر اندر here functions as a local preposition “into/onto/in” that combines with the verb آور “bring”; بخاک is the direction/goal “to the ground” (literally “into the earth/ground”). Word order places object then the light prepositional+verb cluster, typical of older Persian poetic usage.
segments.s700-g91: تودانی is the 2nd‑person present/imperative compound meaning “you know.” که introduces the content clause “that …”. برداد is here taken as the proper name Bardad (a person); نالم is a 1st‑person present/imperfect verb “I lament/complain,” with همی as an iterative adverb marking continuity (“continually”/“ever”). Together: “You know that Bardad I lament continually.”
segments.s700-g92: همه راه literally “every/whole road” — here = “every path.” نیکی is the abstract noun “goodness” or “good.” سگالم is a verbal form meaning roughly “I set dogs on / I harry / I pursue” (a vigorous verb of pursuit or attack); followed again by همی marking continuous/ habitual action. The clause states a habitual activity relative to the ‘paths of goodness.’
segments.s700-g93: تومپسند is a vocative/characterizing phrase built from تو + پسند = “you who like” or “you lover of …” used here as address. بیداد is the noun “injustice/oppression” and functions as the object of پسند (what he likes) or as part of the vocative. بیدادگر is an appositive/epithet meaning “injustice-doer / tyrant.” The three together form a forceful address: “O you who delight in injustice, tyrant.”
segments.s700-g94: بگفت is the past narrative verb “he said” (3rd person). این = “this” (the foregoing words/utterance). و = “and.” بر بست is a verbal phrase: بر (on/onto) + بست (past of بستن, “tied/fastened”) = “he fastened on.” زرین کمر is the noun phrase “golden belt” (زرین adjective before کمر). The sequence describes speech followed by the action of girding the golden belt.
segments.s700-g95: سوی marks direction ('toward') and governs the following noun phrase; دشت دوک is the directional object ('the plain of Duuk'), with دوک functioning here as a name/identifier attached to دشت. اندر آورد روی is a compound verb phrase: آورد is the past 3rd-person singular of 'to bring/cause' and, with اندر, forms the idiom 'اندر آورد روی' = 'turned (his) face toward' (literal: 'brought his face into/on'). The subject is omitted (typical in Persian poetry) and supplied from context ('he').
segments.s700-g96: همی‌شد is an imperfective/past-progressive auxiliary meaning 'was becoming' or 'began to be' (a habitual/progressive past form). خلیده modifies دل ('خلیده‌دل' = 'withdrawn/retiring-hearted') and functions as a predicate describing the subject's inward state; و links that predicate to راه‌جوی, which is an agentive/adjectival noun 'seeker/traveler' describing what he became. Together the clause says his heart turned inward and he became a seeker of the road.
segments.s700-g57: سراپرده ودشت جای وی است = literal order: 'inner-tent and plain place his is.' The sequence places the two coordinated location nouns (سراپرده و دشت) before the copula است; وی is the archaic third-person pronoun used possessively here ('his'). ودشت is written as one token in the data but functions as و + دشت ('and the plain').
segments.s700-g58: نه خرگاه وخیمه سرای وی است = the negator نه precedes the coordinated nouns (خرگاه و خیمه سرای) and negates the following copular clause; سرای here is a noun 'house/abode' (the provided lemma senses are not a perfect match), and وی again marks possession ('his'). The structure is 'it is not X nor Y that is his dwelling.'
segments.s700-g59: فراوان سپاهست بااوبهم = فراوان ('numerous') modifies سپاه; سپاهست is orthographic contraction of سپاه است ('there is/was a host'), so the noun + (copula) expresses 'a great host existed' or 'a large army was (present)'; بااوبهم = با او به‌هم ('together with him') is a prepositional phrase indicating accompaniment and attaches after the verb phrase.
segments.s700-g60: سلیح بزرگی وگنج درم = a noun phrase giving what the army carried: سلیح بزرگی ('great weapons/armaments') coordinated with گنج درم ('treasure in dirhams'); و links the two items. درم here names the coin/money in which the treasure is counted.
segments.s700-g61: کنون تا تو رفتی برین راه بود = کنون تا functions as 'now that / since' introducing a clause of circumstance; تو رفتی is the past tense 'you went'; برین is the fused form بر + این ('to/on that'), so برین راه = 'that road'; بود is the past copula 'was' completing the clause (here used with existential/metrical sense).
segments.s700-g62: نیازش ببرگشتن شاه بود = نیازش ('his need' with the enclitic -ش for possession) is the subject/predicate; ببرگشتن is ب + برگشتن ('for/to the returning'), an infinitival complement introduced by the preposition به (here reduced to ب-), and شاه is the logical subject of that infinitive ('the king'); بود is the past copula 'was' — overall: 'his need was the king's return.'
segments.s700-g63: جهاندار خسرو به موسیل گفت = a nominative phrase with title + name (جهاندار خسرو = 'sovereign Khosrow'); به موسیل is the dative/recipient 'to Musil'; گفت is the past verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses do not list the verbal 'said' sense, so that item is marked below accordingly).
segments.s700-g64: که رنج تو کی ماند اندرنهفت = که introduces reported speech/quotation; the order is interrogative: کی ('when' / 'how long') is fronted for the question, ماند is the verb 'remains', and اندر نهفت (اندرنهفت fused here) means 'hidden/kept within' — syntactically: 'that (he asked) when your suffering would remain hidden.'
segments.s700-g41: هم آنگه is an adverbial phrase (هم + آنگه) giving immediacy/emphasis: 'then/at once.' رسیدند is 3rd-person plural past of رسیدن 'to arrive/approach.' نزدیک شاه is a postverbal locative phrase ('near the king'), with نزدیک used adverbially and شاه the noun it modifies.
segments.s700-g42: پیاده شدند is a compound meaning 'they dismounted/went on foot' (پیاده + شدند, past 3rd plural 'became/on foot'). اندرَان (contracted اندر آن) is a locative adverb 'therein/into that,' and سایه گاه is a compound 'shady-place/spot' (سایه = shade, گاه = place/occasion used here as place).
segments.s700-g43: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when.' رفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they went/approached.' نزدیک خسرو is the locative phrase 'near Khosrow.' فراز is an adverbial/epithet 'on high/aloft,' modifying the preceding phrase (classical poetic order places such modifiers after the noun).
segments.s700-g44: ستودند is past 3rd plural 'they praised.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' بردند here means 'they carried/offered' (took/placed), with نماز as its object: 'they offered prayer.' پیشش is a prepositional adverb 'before/in the presence of him.'
segments.s700-g45: بپرسید is a past narrative form 'he asked.' The following sequence 'خسرو به بندوی گفت' assigns Khosrow as the subject (he asked), and then says 'he spoke to the bondman' (به + indirect object). The verb گفت is the reported-speech verb 'said.'
segments.s700-g46: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing reported speech. گفتم is 1st-person past 'I said.' تو is the object pronoun 'you.' راخاک is a fixed accusative phrase meaning 'to the dust / to abase' (literally 'to dust'); یابم is the 1st-person subjunctive/present form of یافتن used here to express intentional/future action 'I would/find I may find' (classical subjunctive). نهفت is an adjectival/past-participial form 'hidden/concealed' modifying the relevant referent in the reported clause.
segments.s700-g47: به خسرو بگفت is 'he said/told to Khosrow' (به + indirect object, بگفت = he said). آنچ is the relative 'that which/what,' and بر وی رسید is a classical expression 'came upon him / befell him' (بر = upon, وی = him, رسید = befell/arrived).
segments.s700-g48: همان مردمی is 'the same people' (demonstrative + people). کو is a relative/archaic particle 'who/which' introducing the clause; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of,' here linking the people to بهرام (i.e. associated with or sent by Bahram); دید is simple past 'he saw.'
segments.s700-g81: This is a simple clause with the noun subject before the verb: نوان (the young man/youth) + اندر آمد (entered). اندر آمد is the classical/periphrastic past construction meaning "entered/came in," and به آتشکده uses به + noun to mark the destination ('into/to the fire-temple').
segments.s700-g82: دلش بود is the copular clause 'his heart was'; the possessive enclitic ش attaches to دل. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/completely' modifying the predicate phrase. بدرد آژده functions as a predicative phrase meaning 'afflicted/struck with alarm' (بدرد here has a causative/pain sense and combines with آژده 'startled/terrified').
segments.s700-g83: بشد here is a past/perfective verb-form (classical شد) meaning 'he rose/it came to be' applied to the subject; the sequence names the actor (هیربد, 'the chief priest') then the object noun زند ('Zand' — the scriptural/commentarial book) and the verb استا with the adverbial complement بدست ('in the hand'). In poetic prose the noun (زند) can appear before the verb to show what is being held while استا (ایستاد) denotes 'stood' or 'stood holding.'
segments.s700-g84: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/in the presence of' and takes the following nominal phrase جهاندار یزدان‌پرست as its object: 'the sovereign (جهاندار) who is God-worshipping (یزدان‌پرست).' The adjective-like epithet یزدان‌پرست follows the head noun in classical Persian.
segments.s700-g85: گشاد here functions as a verbal/adjectival predicate 'opened/gaped' or 'was opened.' از میان is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the middle of,' and شاه زرین کمر is a noun phrase with زرین qualifying the following کمر (a golden belt/girdle) — literally 'the king's golden belt' or 'the king, with a golden belt.' Classical word order places the descriptive elements after or adjacent as here.
segments.s700-g86: The doubled بر (on/upon) is a poetic feature where بر آتش (upon the fire) is the locative, and آگند here is used as a verbal/adjectival idea 'filled/laid upon' — together meaning they placed/threw (or 'filled') the fire with چندی گهر ('several jewels'). چَندی گهر is the object phrase 'some/several gems.' Word order places the verbal notion before the object phrase in this line's diction.
segments.s700-g87: نیایش کنان is an adverbial participial phrase: نیایش 'prayer' + کنان (the present-participial/gerundive form) meaning 'praying' or 'while praying.' پیش آذر بگشت is a past clause 'he turned/returned toward the fire,' with بگشت the past of گشت 'to turn/return' (here used with به/پیش to indicate direction).
segments.s700-g88: بنالید is a simple past verb 'he moaned/wailed.' وز in classical usage functions as an archaic or contracted preposition/connector (here effectively 'and/from that') introducing the next action. هیربد is again 'the chief priest' as subject of برگذشت, where برگذشت is the past 'passed by' or 'went past.' The clause sequence links the lament to the priest's movement away.

sentences.s700-s53: On that news they were all renewed; with their companions they drew near to Khosrow.
sentences.s700-s49: When he came back to his army at his accustomed hour, the same gloom fell and that night grew long.
sentences.s700-s50: He sent out alert scouts so that they might go and seek news of the world again.
sentences.s700-s51: When the Nimruz host became aware that the world-illuminating king had come by the road.
sentences.s700-s52: They all set (struck/tied) drums on the elephants' backs; the earth became like the sea of the Nile.
sentences.s700-s9: At the place where Mosul stood, there was an Armenian who was a Kurd among my great men.
sentences.s700-s10: In the host there was his companion, the devotee Banduī, who was Banduī Khal, the world-seeker.
sentences.s700-s11: These two circled away from the midst of the host, and Khosrow kept watch of the army on the road.
sentences.s700-s12: To Gostaham the brave one said: 'Thus two horsemen, swift-riding, went into the plain to battle.'
sentences.s700-s37: He thrust one foot out of the saddle; that man, alert of heart, became impatient.
sentences.s700-s38: He kissed the foot and kissed the companion; he stood stunned by the rebuke directed at him.
sentences.s700-s39: When the Khosrow‑worshipping man stood idle, the ambitious man commanded that he take a seat.
sentences.s700-s40: From that plain the ill‑fated steed sprang up and rushed on until before Azar Goshasp.
sentences.s700-s17: What sort of crooked bondman would be coarse — unless the pure God were his ally?
sentences.s700-s18: If you wish to live, be in prison; but if you are to be slain, take the battlefield.
sentences.s700-s19: Gustaham said to him, 'O King, be straight; know this and strike at that muzzle so that that mole is yours.'
sentences.s700-s20: If he comes near and retribution is at hand, do not ask anything of Gustaham the speaker except for your life.
sentences.s700-s5: He set the king's pavilion on the plain; such a host — hungry and on the road — (in) three duk.
sentences.s700-s6: He gave the whole army to Niyātus; they said to him, 'You are the chief of the host.'
sentences.s700-s7: From that place, with the horsemen about him, he handed the reins to Tiztag.
sentences.s700-s8: He turned his face toward the road; he rode on, glad at heart and seeking the way.
sentences.s700-s25: From that time he sought a remedy in those days, and from that his covering was the king's robe.
sentences.s700-s26: He kept saying, and Khosrow wept profusely; then he said to him, 'Who is this man?'
sentences.s700-s27: He said to him, 'O king, sun-faced one, why do you not ask about Mosul's favour?'
sentences.s700-s28: For since you left Iran and I went away, my prosperous dwelling has never slept.
sentences.s700-s1: He adorned my paradise with a sun‑like face; the sun, turning the heavens, made the army comely.
sentences.s700-s2: From the court the sound of the war‑drum rose up; the ebony host was stirred all around.
sentences.s700-s3: He selected troops from the freeborn, and they came toward Azarabadagan.
sentences.s700-s4: For two weeks, by the king's command, an army mustered; the host came again and again, constantly.
sentences.s700-s13: Go toward them and see how long they keep on; in this manner the raiders are gathering for the assault.
sentences.s700-s14: Thus Gostaham said, 'O king, let me drive that piebald horseman away.'
sentences.s700-s15: My brother made him a bondman; that very companion of his is of another army.
sentences.s700-s16: Thus Khosrow, like a lion, said: “Who is this, O brave rider?”
sentences.s700-s33: Let us strive so that your day may become good; may that very name of yours among the great become like the moon.
sentences.s700-s34: He (Mūsil) said to him, 'O king, make my fortunes fresh for me.'
sentences.s700-s35: That I may come and kiss your rākīb, and I may praise your glory and beauty.
sentences.s700-s36: Khosrow said to him: 'By your hardship I will make (it) shine — by these words I will make your treasure glitter.'
sentences.s700-s45: He kept saying, “O just and pure Arbiter, bring the leaders of the enemy to the ground.”
sentences.s700-s46: You know that I lament for Bardad; always I follow every path of goodness.
sentences.s700-s47: “You lover of injustice, tyrant!” he said, and fastened on his golden belt.
sentences.s700-s48: He turned his face toward the plain of Duuk; his heart grew withdrawn and he became a seeker of the road.
sentences.s700-s29: His dwelling is the inner tent and the open plain; it is not a stable or a tented lodging.
sentences.s700-s30: A large host accompanied him, bearing great arms and treasures in dirhams.
sentences.s700-s31: Now that you have gone on that road, his need was for the king to return.
sentences.s700-s32: Sovereign Khosrow said to Musil, 'How long will your suffering remain hidden?'
sentences.s700-s21: Then they came up close to the king and dismounted into that shady spot.
sentences.s700-s22: When they went up near Khosrow they praised him and offered prayer before him.
sentences.s700-s23: Khosrow asked; he said to the bondman, 'that I said I would find you in the dust, hidden.'
sentences.s700-s24: He told Khosrow what had befallen him — the same people whom he had seen from Bahram.
sentences.s700-s41: The young man entered the fire-temple; his heart was completely seized with terror.
sentences.s700-s42: The chief priest took up the Zand and stood, holding it in his hand, before the pious sovereign.
sentences.s700-s43: They opened from the midst of the king's golden belt and cast several jewels upon the fire.
sentences.s700-s44: Praying, he turned toward the fire; he lamented, and the chief priest passed by.
